research_gaps: No comprehensive third-party protocol security audit found despite operating since 2020. Moultrie Audits handles provider-level audits only. Community discussed funding a protocol audit (GitHub Discussion #511) but no completed report is publicly available.
Telegram member count could not be confirmed via web research -- dynamic page did not render
Exact current bonded ratio percentage not explicitly confirmed. Estimated at ~55% based on Messari reports and staking explorer data showing 96.51% 'stake health'.
Q4 2025 granular revenue figure not published separately -- estimated from annual total ($3.15M) minus Q1-Q3 ($2.67M combined)
Starbonds SEC filing status (whether Reg A+ has been formally filed/approved) could not be confirmed. Offering appears to be in progress.
BME activation status as of March 2026 unclear -- slated for 'early Q1 2026' per Q4 2025 report but current live/deploying status not confirmed
Active provider count (current) could not be confirmed -- stats.akash.network did not render. Last confirmed: ~69 (Q1 2025), trending down through 2025
Adam Bozanich current day-to-day involvement level unclear -- single source (Overclock Labs about page) lists him as CTO
Provider count (63-69 active) may fluctuate significantly; no historical provider count data publicly available in time series
Chain deprecation timeline ('late 2026') is aspirational with no published technical roadmap or migration specification
Destination chain for migration not confirmed -- Solana described as 'strong contender' but no governance vote completed
Homenode (beta Feb 2026) adoption metrics not yet available
Number of unique tenants/deployers not publicly tracked or reported
Greg Osuri's personal AKT holdings not publicly disclosed

9 sources (S009-S012, S014, S016-S019) from prior version had null URLs -- aggregated from multiple publications without a single canonical link. Replaced with specific URLs where found in v2 research.

identity.name: Akash Network
identity.one_liner: A permissionless reverse-auction marketplace for cloud compute, positioning as a decentralised alternative to AWS/GCP/Azure with 50-85% cost savings.

founding_team.name: Greg Osuri
founding_team.background: IBM consultant (2006-2008), Kaiser Permanente cloud architect (2008), founded AngelHack (world's largest hackathon org, 100k+ developers across 50 cities), co-founded Overclock Labs (June 2015). Testified before US Congress House Committee on AI energy crisis (May 21, 2025). Contributed to California blockchain legislation. Over 10,000 GitHub contributions.

founding_team.name: Adam Bozanich
founding_team.background: QA Automation Engineer at Symantec (2005), Senior Security Engineer at Mu Dynamics (2006), Software Engineer at Xoopit (2008), Lead Software Architect at CHNL Inc. (2011), independent consultant (2012-2014), co-founded Overclock Labs (2015/2016). US patent holder for network protocol fuzzing. Deep expertise in Go, Kubernetes, security.

rounds.notes: Angel investors included Auren Hoffman and Kevin Lin (Twitch co-founder). Reported by FinSMEs and TechCrunch.
Pre-IEO private round. CoinGecko lists Cypher Capital, Hiro Systems, Cosmostation as investors. Details sparse.
Two rounds on BitMax launchpad: Round 1 at $0.3773, Round 2 at $0.7673. Combined ~$800K. Very small raise by crypto standards.

technical.architecture_summary: Akash is a four-layer architecture built on a Cosmos SDK appchain (v0.53 + CometBFT, as of Mainnet 14 October 2025). Layer 1 (Blockchain): handles deployment orders, bid placement, lease finalisation, payment settlement, and governance via DPoS consensus. Layer 2 (Application): on-chain records for Deployments, Orders, Bids, and Leases with unique identifiers (DSEQ, OSEQ, GSEQ). Layer 3 (Provider): off-chain Provider Daemon software manages interaction with the chain; Kubernetes orchestration runs Docker containers on provider hardware. Layer 4 (User): Akash Console (web), CLI, AkashML API (OpenAI-compatible managed inference at $0.15/M tokens), and SDL (YAML) configuration. The reverse auction mechanism is the core innovation: tenants publish resource requirements, providers bid competitively, lowest qualifying bid wins, and a lease is created on-chain. Actual compute execution is entirely off-chain.
technical.consensus_mechanism: Delegated Proof of Stake (DPoS) via CometBFT (formerly Tendermint). 99 active validators (out of 270 total). ~6 second block time. 167,700 active delegations.
technical.what_is_onchain: Deployment orders, provider bids, reverse auction matching, lease creation, escrow payments, staking/delegation, governance proposals and voting, provider attestation, certificate management, WASM smart contracts (enabled Q4 2025)
technical.what_is_offchain: Actual compute workload execution (Kubernetes containers on provider hardware), GPU inference (AkashML), storage, networking, provider infrastructure management
key_dependencies: Cosmos SDK v0.53 (blockchain framework, upgraded Mainnet 14)
technical.audit_notes: No comprehensive third-party protocol security audit found despite operating since September 2020. CertiK Skynet lists Akash as 'not audited by CertiK'. Moultrie Audits designed a three-tier provider auditing system (bronze/silver/gold) for individual providers -- this is operational/infrastructure auditing, not protocol security auditing. Community GitHub Discussion #511 proposes a security audit/penetration test with $243,701.70 allocated for verifiable computing capabilities (TEE focus), but no completed audit report is publicly available. This is a material risk for production infrastructure with real financial settlements.

security_incidents.description: Critical authentication bypass vulnerability discovered by ChainLight (formerly Theori). An attacker could impersonate a deployment owner, gaining unauthorised access to execute arbitrary commands on any instance. Could leak AI models and training data. Patched and deployed to production by May 1, 2024 (3-day turnaround). Bug bounty paid in AKT. Server-side certificate verification added.

Spam attack: surge of malicious transactions targeting blockchain and Console caused network congestion, temporarily preventing new deployments for several hours. Core team and validators adjusted transaction costs within hours. Contributed to provider attrition in Q2 2025.

token_utility: Staking to validators (secure the network, earn inflation rewards)
Provider incentives (earn AKT for serving workloads)
Take fee revenue (network collects percentage of deployment spend)
BME mechanism (burn AKT to mint ACT compute credits, when activated)

initial_allocation.notes: Majority of max supply distributed through staking rewards over time

initial_allocation.vesting_schedule: Partial at TGE, remainder over 24 months

initial_allocation.notes: Sold at $0.3773/$0.7673 on BitMax (Oct 2020). Very small allocation.

distribution.distribution_method: IEO on BitMax (AscendEX) at $0.3773/$0.7673 per AKT (October 2020). Genesis supply of 100M AKT with breakdown: 34.5% investors, 27% team, 19.7% foundation, 8% ecosystem, 5% testnets, 4% vendors, 1.8% public. Long-term: 70.94% of max supply goes to mining/block rewards. All initial allocations are now fully unlocked. Not a fair launch, but the very modest total raise ($4.1M) and majority mining allocation are notable.
distribution.vesting_status: All tokens fully vested and unlocked as of 2025. No upcoming unlock events.

emissions.halving_schedule: No halving. Genesis inflation was 54% APY, halving every 3.75 years conceptually. Post-Proposal 283 (March 2025): inflation max reduced to 8%, min to 4%. Cosmos SDK adjusts inflation dynamically to target staking ratio.
emissions.burn_mechanism: BME (AEP-76): Approved, implementation slated for Q1 2026. Two-token system: AKT (native staking) + ACT (non-transferable, USD-pegged compute credits). Tenants burn AKT to mint ACT at oracle price; providers receive AKT at settlement price. Price appreciation between top-up and payout creates net deflationary pressure. Circuit breaker at collateral ratio < 0.90. At $10M monthly top-ups: immediately purchases ~8.77M AKT, removes from circulation for ~7 days. At current adoption could reduce effective annual supply inflation from 8% to ~5.2%.

staking.staking_type: Delegated Proof of Stake (nominator staking via Cosmos SDK)
staking_risks: Real yield approximately -0.7% to 0% after inflation (staking APY barely offsets dilution)
21-day unbonding locks capital during volatile periods
Slashing risk: 5% for double-signing, 0.01% for prolonged downtime
Validator selection risk -- poorly-run validators can be slashed
Chain migration risk -- staking mechanics may change with new chain
Post-Proposal 283: 50% community pool tax reduces individual staking returns
staking.slashing_conditions: Double-signing: 5% slash. Extended downtime (missing blocks): 0.01% slash and jailing.
staking.staking_formula_notes: Staking APY = Inflation / Bonded Ratio * (1 - Community Pool Tax). With 8% inflation, ~55% bonded, 50% tax: ~7.3% nominal.

tokenomics.token_model_classification: Inflationary utility token with planned deflationary mechanism (BME). All initial allocations fully unlocked.
tokenomics.tokenomics_notes: AKT currently operates as a standard Cosmos inflationary staking token with real yield near zero after inflation. Post-Proposal 283 (March 2025): inflation max 8%, min 4%, community pool tax 50%. The real tokenomics thesis depends on BME activation (AEP-76, approved, Q1 2026 target): when compute spend is significant enough to burn more AKT than inflation creates, the token becomes deflationary. At $3.15M annual revenue and ~$88M market cap, BME alone cannot drive meaningful deflation yet. All tokens fully vested -- no future unlock cliffs. The gap between the tokenomics vision (revenue-driven burns) and current reality (inflation-dependent staking) is the core investment question. ~96% below ATH. CoinGecko rank #297.

methods.description: Delegate AKT to validators on the Akash chain. Earn inflation rewards (~7.3% APY nominal). Vote on governance proposals through your validator or directly. 50% community pool tax reduces individual returns.
methods.estimated_returns: ~7.3% APY nominal (approximately -0.7% to 0% real yield after inflation and 50% community pool tax)

methods.description: Run an Akash validator node. 99 active validators (of 270 total). Must be in top 99 by stake to be in active set. Earn commission on delegator rewards (typically 5-10%). Top 100 stakers control 63.2% of voting power.
methods.minimum_hardware: Robust server infrastructure, near-100% uptime required
methods.estimated_returns: Commission on delegator rewards (5-10% of staking yield)
barriers_to_entry: Must be in top 99 by stake -- competitive, requires millions of AKT

methods.description: Run a cloud provider node on Akash. List spare compute (CPU, GPU, memory, storage) on the marketplace. Earn AKT from tenant deployments via reverse auction. Permissionless -- anyone with qualifying hardware can join.
methods.minimum_hardware: Minimum: 8 CPU cores, 16GB RAM, 150GB SSD. Recommended: 12+ cores, 48GB+ RAM, 500GB+ SSD, 3 machines. Ubuntu 24.04 LTS, x86_64 only (no ARM). GPU providers need NVIDIA cards. Own RPC node recommended.
methods.estimated_returns: Variable -- depends on hardware, utilisation rate, and bid competitiveness. GPU providers earn more than CPU-only. Provider calculator at akashcalculator.com.

methods.description: Homenode: contribute consumer GPU via simplified provider setup. Early access open beta launched February 25, 2026. Install Homenode OS from USB, boot into it. Dual-boot supported. Phase 1 requires RTX 4090 or RTX 5090 minimum, expanding to RTX 20-50 series, GTX 9-16 series, Quadro.
methods.minimum_hardware: RTX 4090 or RTX 5090 GPU (Phase 1). Consumer PC with USB boot capability.
barriers_to_entry: Limited GPU support in early phases

methods.description: Deploy containerised workloads on Akash as a tenant. Console templates for one-click deployments, or custom SDL (YAML) for advanced configurations. AkashML managed inference at $0.15/M tokens (OpenAI-compatible API). 50-85% cheaper than AWS/GCP/Azure.
methods.estimated_returns: Cost savings vs centralised cloud. Not a yield-generating activity.
barriers_to_entry: AKT or USDC deposit for escrow
No managed services (databases, load balancers, CDN) -- raw compute only

methods.description: Vote on on-chain governance proposals. 300+ proposals to date. Quorum requirement: 33.40%. Recent proposal (#308) had 42.31% turnout. Community has rejected proposals for transparency concerns (Proposal #302).
methods.estimated_returns: No direct returns. Influence over protocol direction.

methods.description: Build on or integrate with Akash. Four funding mechanisms: Community Pool (on-chain treasury, 50% of inflation), Akash Accelerate (grants), Akash Bounties Board, AkashML partnerships. Student Ambassador Program at Princeton, Cornell, USC, UT Austin.
methods.estimated_returns: Grant funding available. No direct token emissions to builders.
barriers_to_entry: Must propose to community pool via governance or apply to grants programme

developer_resources.developer_community_size: 350+ GitHub contributors across 65 repos. Core node repo has 34 contributors. Student Ambassador Program at major universities.

usage_metrics.compute_delivered: ~69 active providers (Q1 2025, declining through year), ~700-1,000 GPUs (NVIDIA), CPU/memory/storage. 3.1M deployments created in 2025 (466% growth from 2024). 126,025 new leases (2.2% growth). AkashML managed inference operational since November 2025. ~60% GPU utilisation (self-reported).
usage_metrics.revenue_source: Take fee on deployment spend. $3.15M total USD spend in 2025 (128% YoY growth). 1.94M AKT network spend (467% growth). $806,726 USDC spend (184% growth). Average fee per lease: $25.03 (127% increase). Daily revenue peak: $13,000+.
usage_metrics.metrics_source: Akash 2025 Year in Review, Messari Q1-Q3 2025 quarterly reports, Akash Q4 2025 Report
usage_metrics.usage_notes: Quarterly breakdown from Messari: Q1 $1M revenue / 868 daily active leases / 553 GPU avg; Q2 $820K / 659 daily leases / 370 GPU avg (post-spam-attack decline); Q3 $852K / 367 GPU avg. Key customers: Venice.ai (GPU inference, billions of tokens), ElizaOS (default inference via AkashChat API), Morpheus (one-click Console template since April 2025), Gensyn (RL-Swarm H100 nodes since May 2025). Revenue of $3.15M against ~$88M market cap gives price-to-revenue ratio of ~28x. Provider count declining through 2025 despite usage growth -- spam attack and market downturn hurt provider economics. Homenode (Feb 2026 beta) aims to reverse this trend.

community.governance_participation_rate: Active. 300+ on-chain proposals. Quorum 33.40%. Proposal #308 had 42.31% turnout with 100% 'Yes'. 8 proposals passed in Q1 2025. Community rejected Proposal #302 (Akash Adoption Lab) for lack of transparency -- healthy governance signal.
community.governance_model: Cosmos SDK on-chain governance. Token-weighted voting with delegation. Any AKT holder can submit proposals (with deposit). Validators vote on behalf of delegators by default; delegators can override. Quorum: 33.40%. 50% community pool tax (post-Proposal 282, March 2025).
key_community_channels: Akash Network Stats (stats.akash.network) -- live marketplace data
community.sentiment_summary: Community is technically oriented and practically focused. Strong conviction around the 'decentralised supercloud' thesis. GPU marketplace and AkashML re-energised interest. Key concerns: 96% price decline from ATH, chain migration uncertainty, declining provider count through 2025, BME activation delay. Venice.ai's adoption as a customer is frequently cited as validation. Community frustrated by the gap between narrative ('decentralised AWS') and current scale (69 providers, 700 GPUs). Proposal #302 rejection showed governance has teeth.
community.community_notes: Greg Osuri testified before US Congress (May 2025) -- first decentralised cloud founder to do so. Student Ambassador Program at Princeton, Cornell, USC, UT Austin. Akash Accelerate event (June 2025) drew 500 attendees. Twitter handle is @akashnet_ (not @akaborNetwork as some sources report). Grayscale added AKT to AI Tools & Resources sector index (May 2025). AKT included in Coinbase 50 Index (June 2025).

infrastructure_decentralisation.evidence: 99 active validators (top 100 stakers control 63.2% of voting power). ~69 active providers globally with permissionless onboarding -- anyone with qualifying hardware can join. ~700-1,000 GPUs deployed across independent providers. Geographic distribution is real. However: 69 providers is objectively thin for a 'cloud marketplace'. Provider count declined through 2025. Starcluster/Starbonds introduces centralisation: protocol-owned compute at enterprise datacentres with 'vetted Nodekeepers'. Homenode (Feb 2026 beta) is a decentralising force but very early. Chain migration to potentially Solana introduces uncertainty about future validator model. Overclock Labs remains the dominant development entity.

governance_decentralisation.evidence: 300+ on-chain governance proposals with active participation. Proposal #308 had 42.31% turnout, exceeding 33.4% quorum. 8 proposals passed in Q1 2025 alone. Community rejected Proposal #302 (Akash Adoption Lab) for lack of transparency -- governance has real teeth. 50% community pool tax funds community-directed spending. However: Overclock Labs drives the strategic roadmap. Chain migration was announced by founder, not governance-voted. Greg Osuri is the primary public voice and decision-maker on major strategic direction. Starbonds appears to be a company decision, not community-governed.

token_distribution_fairness.evidence: Genesis: 34.5% investors, 27% team -- heavily insider-weighted at launch. Long-term: 70.94% of max supply goes to mining/block rewards -- good. All tokens fully vested/unlocked -- no future dilution cliffs. Very modest fundraise ($4.1M total, with IEO component of only $800K). IEO was small with reasonable pricing ($0.3773-$0.7673). Current price (-96% from ATH) means many original investors have lost significantly. Not a fair launch, but the modest fundraise and majority mining allocation are above average.

censorship_resistance.evidence: Permissionless provider and tenant participation. No KYC required to deploy or provide. Venice.ai integration demonstrates privacy-preserving compute. Reverse auction is on-chain and transparent. However: Starbonds/Starcluster introduces 'vetted' Nodekeepers -- potential censorship vector at the enterprise GPU layer. Overclock Labs is US-incorporated (Delaware) -- susceptible to US regulatory pressure. Spam attack (March 2025) showed network can be disrupted temporarily. Chain migration adds uncertainty about future censorship properties.

data_sovereignty.evidence: Workloads run on provider hardware -- tenants control their deployment configurations and data. No central data collection by Akash protocol. SDL-based deployment gives users full control over infrastructure choices. Self-custodial Cosmos wallets (Keplr/Leap). Provider auditing (Moultrie) does not inspect tenant data. Homenode allows users to control their own hardware. However: providers physically host workloads and could theoretically inspect container contents. No encryption at compute layer. No zero-knowledge or confidential computing. ChainLight vulnerability (patched) showed potential for unauthorised deployment access. AkashML managed inference adds convenience but reduces sovereignty (managed service with centralised API).

open_source_transparency.evidence: Fully open source under Apache 2.0 licence across 65 repositories. Active development with real commits (Mainnet 14 was a massive Cosmos SDK v0.45 to v0.53 migration). All governance proposals public on GitHub. Quarterly reports published via Messari and internal blog. On-chain governance is transparent and verifiable. Revenue and provider metrics publicly accessible. Community development funded from community pool (50% of inflation). Build process uses standard Go toolchain. However: no formal protocol-level security audit despite 5+ years of operation. Overclock Labs' corporate financials not public. Starbonds introduces a new opaque financial dimension.

freedom_score.freedom_summary: Akash scores a C (66/100), reflecting genuine infrastructure decentralisation with functional governance and exemplary open-source practices. The permissionless provider model and active on-chain governance (300+ proposals, community rejecting proposals for transparency) are strong points. Open-source transparency is near-exemplary at 13/15. However, the small provider set (~69, declining), insider-heavy genesis distribution, upcoming chain migration uncertainty, Starcluster centralisation vectors, and US jurisdiction of Overclock Labs prevent a higher score. The absence of a formal security audit for 5+ year production infrastructure is a material gap. If Akash successfully executes chain migration, activates BME, and grows provider diversity via Homenode, the score would improve materially.

what_works_today: Working cloud marketplace with 3.1M deployments and $3.15M revenue in 2025 (128% YoY growth) from real customers
Permissionless provider model -- anyone with qualifying hardware can participate
50-85% cheaper than centralised cloud for comparable compute
AkashML managed inference ($0.15/M tokens, OpenAI-compatible) simplifies AI workload deployment
Named customers: Venice.ai (GPU inference), ElizaOS (default inference provider), Morpheus (Console template), Gensyn (RL-Swarm H100 nodes)
Active on-chain governance: 300+ proposals, 42.31% turnout, community rejected a proposal for transparency concerns
Fully open source (Apache 2.0) with 65 repos, 350+ contributors, and genuine development activity
Greg Osuri's credibility: AngelHack founder, testified before US Congress on AI energy
Coinbase 50 Index and Grayscale AI sector index inclusion (2025)
Homenode (Feb 2026 beta) aims to expand provider base to consumer hardware
what_is_hype: 'Decentralised Supercloud/AWS' narrative is aspirational -- ~69 providers with ~700 GPUs is not comparable to AWS's scale
60% GPU utilisation sounds strong but the absolute base is tiny (~700 GPUs vs millions at hyperscalers)
Chain migration framed as 'evolution' but is effectively rebuilding the entire infrastructure layer with no published technical specification
Revenue growth (128% YoY) is from a very small base ($1.4M to $3.15M) -- not yet meaningful at infrastructure scale
Starbonds ($75M securities offering) framed as community-aligned but is a corporate fundraise by Overclock Labs with undisclosed yields
BME deflationary narrative is premature -- current revenue cannot drive meaningful burns relative to 8% inflation
Staking yield (~7.3% nominal) presented as attractive but real yield is approximately -0.7% to 0% after inflation and 50% community pool tax
key_risks: Chain deprecation (late 2026) is the single highest-impact risk -- migrating an entire blockchain and marketplace is technically complex and could fracture the community. No published technical specification, no governance vote on destination chain.
~69 active providers is not meaningfully decentralised for a cloud marketplace. Provider count declined through 2025 despite usage growth.
No formal protocol security audit despite 5+ years of production operation -- ChainLight auth bypass vulnerability (May 2024) and spam attack (March 2025) demonstrate real attack surface
96% below all-time high ($0.30 vs $8.08) -- sustained price decline undermines provider incentives and community morale

Overclock Labs is a US-incorporated entity (Delaware) -- regulatory exposure to SEC, CFTC, and compute export controls. Greg Osuri's congressional testimony is proactive engagement but also increases regulatory visibility.
Greg Osuri key person risk -- he is the public face, strategic driver, congressional witness, and credibility anchor. No documented succession planning.
BME activation delay -- the core deflationary mechanism has been 'coming soon' for years, now slated for Q1 2026
Starbonds ($75M) introduces regulatory and dilution risk -- Reg A+ terms not fully public, yields undisclosed due to SEC requirements
Spam attack (March 2025) demonstrated network resilience gaps and contributed to provider attrition
Starcluster introduces enterprise-centralised compute layer ('vetted Nodekeepers') that could undermine the permissionless thesis
competitive_advantages: Longest-running decentralised cloud marketplace -- operating since September 2020 with continuous mainnet since launch
Truly permissionless provider onboarding -- structural differentiator from Render (permissioned) and most competitors
Cost advantage (50-85% cheaper) addresses real pain point. AkashML at $0.15/M tokens undercuts most inference providers.
Greg Osuri's verifiable open-source credibility (AngelHack, AnyCast) and institutional engagement (congressional testimony)
Very modest fundraise ($4.1M total) means less VC overhang than heavily-funded competitors
Cosmos SDK provides battle-tested infrastructure with IBC interoperability
Active governance with real community participation and accountability
competitive_disadvantages: ~69 providers vs thousands of data centres at hyperscalers -- scale gap is enormous and provider count declined in 2025
No managed services (databases, load balancers, CDN) -- raw compute only, limiting enterprise adoption
Chain migration will reset much of the technical momentum built on Cosmos
Cosmos ecosystem shrinking in mindshare relative to Ethereum L2s and Solana
No security audit for production infrastructure -- competitive disadvantage vs audited alternatives
Raw compute is a commodity -- differentiation relies on cost and permissionless access, both of which larger competitors can eventually match
comparable_projects: Render Network (RENDER) -- decentralised GPU rendering and compute (permissioned model, proprietary core)
io.net -- decentralised GPU network (newer entrant, competing for same market)
Aethir -- decentralised cloud gaming and AI compute
Nosana -- decentralised GPU compute on Solana
assessment.outlook: Akash occupies a credible position as the longest-running permissionless cloud marketplace with real revenue and recognisable customers. The next 12-18 months are make-or-break: BME activation (Q1 2026), chain migration execution (late 2026), and Homenode scaling will determine whether the 'decentralised supercloud' thesis is viable or aspirational. Greg Osuri's congressional testimony signals institutional ambition. Starbonds ($75M for 7,200 GB200 GPUs) is a bet on enterprise-grade capacity, but introduces centralisation risk through 'vetted Nodekeepers'. The fundamental question: can a permissionless marketplace with 69 providers capture meaningful share from hyperscalers? The revenue growth (128% YoY) is encouraging but from a tiny base. For practitioners: staking offers near-zero real yield. Providing compute is the more interesting participation mode. Homenode lowers barriers if you have qualifying hardware. The investment thesis depends on chain migration success and BME activation -- both are execution bets, not narrative bets.
assessment.overall_assessment: Akash is the most credible permissionless cloud marketplace operating today, with real revenue ($3.15M), real customers (Venice.ai, ElizaOS, Morpheus, Gensyn), and a founder who has testified before Congress. The open-source commitment is exemplary (65 repos, Apache 2.0, active development). However, the gap between narrative ('decentralised Supercloud') and reality (~69 providers, ~700 GPUs, $3.15M revenue) remains substantial. The planned chain deprecation is a high-risk strategic bet. No formal security audit after 5+ years is indefensible for production infrastructure. Provider count declining despite usage growth suggests the economics aren't yet compelling enough to attract capacity. For the OYM thesis: Akash demonstrates that permissionless decentralised compute can work at a functional level and serve real customers, but it has not yet proven it can work at meaningful scale.
